topic: Someone receives a friend request from a childhood bully
raw_output:
[story 1]

The notification blinked on my screen, a stark, unwelcome intrusion into the quiet hum of my evening. A profile picture loaded: a grinning face I hadn't seen in fifteen years, the face of Mark, the architect of so many awkward lunchtime humiliations. A friend request. My jaw tightened, a muscle jumping near my temple. I stared at the screen, my knuckles turning white where I gripped my mug. How dare he? After all the years of distance, the silence, the fact that he’d spent the better part of my formative years trying to make me feel small. I didn't even bother to tap ‘Accept.’ Instead, I minimized the app, took a deep, shaky breath, and walked over to the window, watching the streetlights blur. The air felt suddenly thick, heavy with a simmering resentment that I desperately tried to keep banked down.

[story 2]

"Seriously?" I muttered under my breath, the sound barely audible above the traffic outside. The message from Mark was simple: a request to reconnect. My stomach clenched, a tight, unpleasant knot forming just beneath my ribs. I felt a flush creep up my neck, hot and furious. It was a deliberate provocation, a reach back into a past I had meticulously walled off. I leaned back in my chair, the cheap plastic groaning under my weight, and let out a short, sharp exhale. I didn't want to engage. I didn't want to give him the satisfaction of seeing any reaction. My fingers hovered over the phone, trembling slightly, before I deliberately locked the screen and placed the device face down on the desk, refusing to acknowledge the digital trespass.

[story 3]

I reread the request three times, my eyes scanning the familiar, mocking layout of his profile. A slow burn started low in my chest, spreading outward like a slow-moving fever. It was absurd. After everything, after the years spent trying to build a life completely separate from his shadow, this was the expected move? I stood up abruptly, pacing the length of my small living room. My movements were jerky, fueled by a rising tide of sharp disapproval. I picked up my phone, my thumb hovering over the delete button, a phantom urge to erase the entire interaction. The sheer audacity of the gesture made my throat feel tight, ready to snap. I finally tossed the phone onto the sofa cushion with unnecessary force, the sound echoing the sudden, restless energy vibrating through my frame.
